coredata.dc:title: Controlled synthesis of spindle-shaped terrylenediimide nanoparticles for enhanced tumor accumulation and treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shuang
Wang, Xu
Qiao, Yanjun
Gao, Baoxiang
Zheng, Min
Xie, Zhigang
coredata.dc:description: The shape tunability of organic nanomaterials is a challenging research topic, moreover, the studies involving shape variation on their photothermal efficiency have been never reported. Herein, the spherical (TSTDI@PEG-PLA) and spindle (TSTDI@F-127) nanoparticles (NPs) were controlly synthesized by assembling a terrylenediimide derivative (named as TSTDI) with amphiphilic copolymers PEG5k-PLA2K and F-127, respectively. Of note, the nanospindles exhibit higher cellular uptake, preferred tumor accumulation, and improved photothermal treatment effect, compared to the nanospheres. The shape tunability of terrylenediimide-based nanomaterials demonstrate the great potential of nonspherical nanomaterials in cancer treatment, and provide new strategies for designing efficient photothermal agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perylenediimide
Spindle-shaped nanoparticles
Spherical nanoparticles
Morphology
Photothermal therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterizations of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs
Cellular uptake and in vitro photothermal therapy of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs
In vivo antitumor activity of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of a) TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and b) TSTDI@F-127 NPs. Insets: Size distribution of a) TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and b) TSTDI@F-127 NPs determined by DLS. c) UV-vis absorption and d) fluorescence spectra of TSTDI in DMF, TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs. Insets in Fig. 1c and d: photographs of TSTDI in DMF (left), TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs (middle) and TSTDI@F-127 NPs (right) under c) natural light d) 645 nm illumination.

Temperature change curves of a) TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and b) TSTDI@F-127 NPs with time upon irradiation (685 nm, 0.8 W cm−2). c) Temperature change curves of TSTDI@F-127 NPs at various laser power densities. d) Photothermal reproducibility of TSTDI@F-127 NPs (20 μg mL−1), under laser irradiation (685 nm, 0.8 W cm−2), over 4 cycles of heating–cooling. Photothermal conversion effect of e) TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and f) TSTDI@F-127 NPs under laser irradiation (685 nm, 0.8 W cm−2) and the laser was switched off after 10 min.

Cell viabilities of HeLa cells treated with TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs or TSTDI@F-127 NPs a) without or b) with irradiation (685 nm, 0.8 W cm−2, 10 min). c) Fluorescence images of HeLa cells co-stained with Calcein-AM and PI, after incubated with TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs or TSTDI@F-127 NPs at the equivalent concentration of TSTDI, and treated with irradiation. Scale bars, 100 μm. Data are expressed as mean SD (n = 3). ***P < 0.001 and *P < 0.05.

a) Florescence imaging of U14 tumor-bearing mice via intravenous injection of BDP-labeled TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs (0.74 mg kg−1). b) Quantitative analysis of the mean florescence signal at tumor sites c) Infrared thermal images of U14 tumor-bearing mice via intravenous injection of PBS, TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs, respectively. d) Temperature changes in tumor sites. Data are expressed as mean SD (n = 3). ***P < 0.001, **P < 0.01 and *P < 0.05.

a) Body weight and b) relative tumor volume post treatment with PBS, TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs. c) Average tumor weights on the 10th day. d) Representative tumor images on the 10th day. e) H&E staining of tumor tissues from mice with various treatments, scale bar: 100 μm. Data are expressed as mean SD (n = 4). ***P < 0.001.
Synthetic route to TSTDI: (i) Bromine, Chloroform, 70 °C, 12 h; (ii) 4-isopropylbenzenethiol, KOH, NMP, N2, 80 °C, 15 h.
Schematic illustration of TSTDI@PEG-PLA spherical and TSTDI@F127 spindle NPs for the comparison of their PTT effects.

head.ce:title: Controlled synthesis of spindle-shaped terrylenediimide nanoparticles for enhanced tumor accumulation and treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis, (Ministry of Education), and Key Laboratory of Analytical Science and Technology of Hebei Province, College of Chemistry and Environment, Science, Hebei Province, College of Chemistry and Environment, Science, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The shape tunability of organic nanomaterials is a challenging research topic, moreover, the studies involving shape variation on their photothermal efficiency have been never reported. Herein, the spherical (TSTDI@PEG-PLA) and spindle (TSTDI@F-127) nanoparticles (NPs) were controlly synthesized by assembling a terrylenediimide derivative (named as TSTDI) with amphiphilic copolymers PEG5k-PLA2K and F-127, respectively. Of note, the nanospindles exhibit higher cellular uptake, preferred tumor accumulation, and improved photothermal treatment effect, compared to the nanospheres. The shape tunability of terrylenediimide-based nanomaterials demonstrate the great potential of nonspherical nanomaterials in cancer treatment, and provide new strategies for designing efficient photothermal agents.

ce:section.ce:para: Photothermal therpay (PTT) is one kind of local treatment strategy for tumors or other diseases, which induce the selective hyperthermic cells death, through converting light energy to heat by photosensitizers [1–9]. In recent decades, scientists have devoted great enthusiasm to developing variety of PTT agents, in order to achieve improved therapeutic outcomes. Most of the photosensitizers are inorganic nanomaterials [10–13] (e.g., noble metal nanostructures [14–16] and carbon nanomaterials [17–20]). Despite the relatively high PTT efficiency, the biodegradation of these inert nanomaterials has remained a tough task to be accomplished, which is a big stumbling block for their clinical translation. In this respect, organic fluorophores possess the advantages of biodegradability and biocompatibility, which are ideal alternatives to inorganic nanomaterials. Organic dyes have been extensively utilized as PTT agents for disease therapy [21,22]. Furthermore, their nanoscale formulations usually can enhance the solubility, stability and bioavailability [23].

Up to date, inorganic nanomaterials with a variety of nonspherical shapes including nanorods, nanocages, nanoshells and nanostars etc.. have been reported [24–28], whose geometrical shapes could affect their fate in living body, especially the blood circulation and tumor accumulation [29–32]. By contrast, almost all of the organic nanoparticles are spherical, fabricating shape-tunable organic nanomaterials remains a huge challenge [33–35]. On the other hand, although extensive efforts have been devoted to improve the photothermal conversion efficiency of organic nanomaterials, revealing the relationship between the morphology and their biological behaviors are seldom explored. Therefore, the study on tunable shapes of organic PTT agents and their morphology effect on bio-behaviors is of significance.

Terrylenediimides, the well-known near-infrared organic dyes, exhibit attractive merits of high extinction coefficients, brilliant color, high thermal, chemical and photochemical stability [36–39], which have promising potential in organic photoelectric devices [40], sensing [41] and supramolecular assemblies [42]. Compared with other organic fluorophores (e.g., BODIPY [43–48]), TDIs acting as PTT agents for cancer therapy is rarely reported [49]. Furthermore, studies involving shape variation on PTT efficiency of TDI-based nanoparticles have never been exploited yet. Herein, a terrylenediimide derivative (TSTDI) was synthesized through a multi-step process (Scheme 1 ). To our surprise, when poly(ethylene glycol)-block-poly(L-lactic acid) (PEG5k-PLA2K) and pluronic F-127 (F-127) were utilized as carriers to assemble with TSTDI, two different shapes of nanoparticles (NPs) could be obtained as spherical (TSTDI@PEG-PLA) and spindle (TSTDI@F-127) NPs, respectively. Significantly, the nanospindles exhibit enhanced cellular uptake and tumor accumulation, and higher tumor inhibition rate in comparison to the nanospheres, which can be utilized as an effective PTT agent for cancer treatment.

As depicted in Scheme 1, terrylenedicarboximide (TDI) was prepared according to the literature procedures [50]. Tetrabromoterrylenetetracarboxdiimide (TBTDI) was prepared by bromination of TDI with six equivalents of bromine in chloroform at 70 °C under argon for 24 h. Prevention of bromine volatilization is the key to obtain TBTDI with high yield. In the next step, TSTDI was synthesized through complete exchange of the four bromine groups of TBTDI by 4-isopropylbenzenethiol (10 equivalents) with eight equivalents of KOH in N-methylpyrrolidone at 80 °C over 36 h. TSTDI shows good solubility in common organic solvents such as cyclohexane, dichloromethane, chloroform, tetrahydrofuran, dimethyl sulfoxide, and N,N-dimethylformamide. The chemical structure of TSTDI was validated by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR, Figs. S1 and S2) and matrix assisted laser desorption/ionization-time of flight mass spectroscopy (MALDI-TOF MS, Figs. S3–S6). The nanoparticle formulations were made by the nanoprecipitation method. The THF solution of TSTDI and PEG5k-PLA2K or F-127 was added to water dropwise under stirring for 12 h. Then, the resulting dispersion was dialyzed against water. The obtained spherical and spindle NPs were named as TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs, respectively (Scheme 2 ). Since PEG5k-PLA2K is a diblock copolymer, and PLA is hydrophobic segment, while F127 is a triblock copolymer with polypropylene glycol as hydrophobic block. The different shapes of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs mainly result from the interaction between TSTDI and the hydrophobic part of the copolymers, similar result was observed in our previous work [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image reveals the spherical structure of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and their average diameter is 66 nm (Fig. 1 a), and the hydrodynamic diameter is around 176.0 nm with polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.095 (inset in Fig. 1a). The size exhibits a slight increase within 7 d (Fig. S7). Fig. 1b demonstrates the size and morphology of TSTDI@F-127 NPs, and the hydrodynamic diameter is around 196.3 nm. TEM image shows the spindle morphology with the average size of 160 nm in length. TSTDI@F-127 NPs can remain stable in water within 7 d and maintain the spindle morphology (Fig. S8). Moreover, the stabilities of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs in phosphate buffered saline with 10% fetal bovine serum were validated by the unchanged size for 48 h (Fig. S9). Compared with TSTDI, TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs have a wider absorption band in NIR region (Fig. 1c). Moreover, a new peak present at 765 nm validates the formation of TSTDI aggregates. Unlike TSTDI@F-127 NPs, TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs exhibit a small red-shift versus TSTDI, revealing the different formation of aggregate. The fluorescence quantum yield of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs is 0.2% and 0.7%, respectively. Thus, no obvious fluorescence could be observed for the aqueous solution of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs (Fig. 1d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and in vitro photothermal therapy of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The photothermal capability of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs were evaluated, by varying their concentrations (2.5 to 20 μg mL−1). As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs exhibited similar concentration-dependent temperature elevation under 685 nm laser (0.8 W cm−2). As for 20 μg mL−1 of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs or TSTDI@F-127 NPs, the temperature could reach by 34 °C within 10 min. As a control, the temperature of water was hardly changed. Moreover, the degree of temperature rise of TSTDI@F-127 NPs depends on the intensity of laser (Fig. 2c). In addition, we assessed the changes of temperature of heating and cooling for four circulations, the temperature of TSTDI@F-127 NPs kept consistent (Fig. 2d), indicating TSTDI@F-127 NPs possess excellent photothermal conversion performance and high photothermal stability. The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs were 32.5% (Figs. 2e and S10a) and 33.4% (Figs. 2f and S10b), respectively [51–54]. Thus, TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs possess similar photothermal efficiency in aqueous solution. Subsequently, the absorbance changes of TSTDI, TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs under laser irradiation were recorded. No obvious declines were observed after irradiation for 30 min (Fig. S11), confirming they have good photostabilities.

The cellular internalization of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs were estimated by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). Diketopyrrolopyrrole (BDP) was chosen as a probe and BDP-encapsulated TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs were prepared (see Supporting Information) for bioimaging [45]. Human cervical carcinoma (HeLa) cells were incubated with BDP-labeled TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs or TSTDI@F-127 NPs for 3 h or 6 h, red fluorescence enhanced from 3 to 6 h (Fig. S12), indicating the time-dependent endocytic behaviors of the two types of NPs. Moreover, the mean fluorescence intensity of cells treated by TSTDI@F-127 NPs is stronger than that of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs, verifying a higher cellular uptake of TSTDI@F-127 NPs. The larger surface area introduces the greater adhesion and would enhance the interaction with cell membrane, which would stimulate macropinocytosis pathway [55–57]. Spherical nanoparticles have only one face that can interact with the cell surface, while spidle-like nanoparticles have multiple faces with large variations in size in each dimension. Therefore, TSTDI@F-127 NPs obviously have a larger surface area than TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs, resulting in higher cellular uptake.

To assess the PTT effects of NPs with two different morphologies, the cytotoxicity of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs toward HeLa cells were investigated by 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs show negligible cytotoxicity in the absence of irradiation. However, their photothermal toxicities were significantly different, with half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) value of 11.9 and 7.0 μg mL−1 for TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs, respectively (Fig. 3b), demonstrating that the spindle shape of TSTDI@F-127 NPs could induce the potent cytotoxicity upon irradiation. This difference in phototoxicity was ascribed to the easy internalization of spindle-shaped NPs. To further visualize the PTT effect in vitro, the cells were stained by calcein AM (live cells, green) and propidium iodide (dead cells, red) (Fig. 3c) after laser exposure. In the PBS groups, all of the cells glowed bright green fluorescence, suggesting negligible light damage. When the concentration of TSTDI was 15 μg mL−1, a significant cell death was observed in the TSTDI@F-127 NPs group, by contrast, the majority of cells in TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs group remained alive. As the concentration of TSTDI increased to 20 μg mL−1, all of the cells in the two groups were killed, indicating this photoinduced cytotoxicity was in a concentration-dependent manner. The results were consistent with those of MTT assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antitumor activity of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The biological behaviors of these two kinds of NPs were further investigated in living mice by the tumor accumulation and treatment. First, in vivo biodistribution of BDP-labeled TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs were assessed in U14 tumor bearing mouse. Both TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs can accumulate in tumor post 3 h of intravenous injection (Fig. 4 a and b). Along with the extension of time, the fluorescence signal of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs decreased gradually, while the fluorescence signal of TSTDI@F-127 NPs increased gradually, TSTDI@F-127 NPs still remained strong fluorescent signals at the tumor sites even after 24 h. Furthermore, TSTDI@F-127 NPs presented much stronger tumor locating capability than TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs. Subsequently, we explored the tumor treatment effects of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs. Kunming mice with U14 tumor xenografts (about 100 mm3) were injected with PBS, TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs or TSTDI@F-127 NPs (1.19 mg kg−1), respectively, via the tail vein. Six hours later, the tumor sites were irradiated (685 nm, 0.8 W cm−2) for 10 min. Infrared thermal imager was utilized to record the temperature changes of tumor sites, which also could reflect content of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs or TSTDI@F-127 NPs in tumors. As shown in Fig. 4c and 4d, the temperature of the PBS + light irradiation (L), TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs + L, TSTDI@F-127 NPs + L groups increased to 39.6, 43.0 and 46.6 °C, respectively, after irradiation for 10 min. The tumors with the injection of TSTDI@F-127 NPs + L showed a higher temperature enhancement than that of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs + L group, demonstrating a better tumor accumulation capacity of TSTDI@F-127 NPs.

Then, the tumor volumes were measured in the following 10 d. In PBS + L group, tumor volumes increased significantly. By contrast, TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs + L and TSTDI@F-127 NPs + L could remarkably suppress the tumor growth, and the tumor weight was also reduced dramatically. It is worthy to note that the tumor inhibition rate (Fig. S13) of TSTDI@F-127 NPs + L reaches up to 95.9%, which is much higher than that of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs + L (68.6%), confirming that TSTDI@F-127 NPs + L possesses stronger tumor inhibition ability than TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs + L (Fig. 5 b-d). The tumor tissues were subjected to Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining (Fig. 5e), the necrotic degree of the tumor in TSTDI@F-127 NPs + L group is more severe than that of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs + L group, further confirming TSTDI@F-127 NPs + L could efficiently inhibit the proliferation of tumor tissues. In addition, the body weights of the mice were unaffected by various treatments (Fig. 5a). H&E staining of major organs were depicted in Fig. S14, no significant tissue abnormality was observed in the three groups, meaning that TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs have good biocompatibility. The complete blood panels (Fig. S15a) and serum biochemistry (Fig. S15b) also revealed the good biosafety of TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs and TSTDI@F-127 NPs.

In summary, the spherical and spindle NPs were controlled fabricated, through the assembling of TSTDI with PEG5k-PLA2K (TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs) and F-127 (TSTDI@F-127 NPs), respectively. Relative to spherical TSTDI@PEG-PLA NPs, spindle TSTDI@F-127 NPs exhibited enhanced cellular uptake and phototoxicity under the same conditions. For in vivo experiments, the spindle TSTDI@F-127 NPs could accumulate at tumor sites more efficiently than the spherical counterparts, and then exert more effective inhibition of tumor growth. Accompanied with the excellent tumor accumulation ability and biocompatibility, TSTDI@F-127 NPs can be harnessed as a potential candidate for cancer treatment. This work provides a new strategy for the development of unique organic nanomaterials.
All animal experiments comply with the ARRIVE guidelines and the British Animal (Scientific Procedures) Act 1986 and related guidelines.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (No. 51873023, 51973214 and 21274036), the Jilin Province Science and Technology Research Project, China (No. 20200201088JC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.129552.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
